narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a white male applicant with a solid middle-income family background and plans to pursue full-time studies, you've put real effort into your law school application, and we appreciate the journey that's brought you here. Your undergraduate GPA of 2.90 sits a bit below the typical 3.21 average for students in our dataset, while your Year 1 school decile ranking of 8th—well above the average 5.71—shows you started strong academically. However, your LSAT score of 21.50 is notably lower than the dataset average of 36.70, and your Year 3 decile of 4th dipped below the typical 5.53. The admissions model, which predicts the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated your probability of failure at 45%, tipping the scales just enough to result in a rejection of your application. This prediction was driven primarily by your LSAT score, which had the strongest influence in pushing the model toward forecasting a bar failure. On a more positive note, your impressive Year 1 decile ranking pulled significantly in the opposite direction, suggesting toward a higher chance of passing, followed by your undergraduate GPA, which also helped counterbalance the risk. Your Year 3 decile had a smaller nudge toward failure, while your full-time status and middle family income bracket offered minor boosts toward success. Compared to typical students who pass the bar, your standout early grades align with their profiles, but the lower LSAT and later decile drop set you apart in ways that raised concerns for the model. While this outcome is disappointing, it highlights clear areas like LSAT preparation where targeted improvement could shift future predictions in your favor. We encourage you to consider retaking the LSAT to boost that score closer to the average, perhaps while strengthening your later academic performance through additional coursework or tutoring. Many applicants in similar situations have succeeded by addressing these key factors and reapplying—your strong Year 1 foundation gives you a great starting point. Keep pushing forward; your determination as a dedicated full-time student will serve you well.
